SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper’s main claims are clearly stated and supported: a theoretical derivation for the SIF weighting (a/(a+p(w))) is provided via a generative model, and extensive experiments across 22 datasets with appropriate baselines (avg-GloVe, tfidf, PARAGRAM, RNN/LSTM variants, etc.) show consistent improvements and robustness analyses. The mathematical derivation is presented and used to set the weighting, but the summary does not allow full verification of completeness, so I treat it as plausible rather than perfect. Experimental methodology is sound overall (multiple tasks, metrics, and baselines), and the authors explicitly acknowledge limitations (ignoring word order, weaker on sentiment/rare tasks). No major logical gaps are evident from the summary, so only minor issues remain that do not undermine the main conclusions.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is very well organized with a logical flow from introduction to method (SIF weighting and projection removal) to theoretical justification and extensive experiments across many datasets and baselines. The main contributions are stated early, notation and Algorithm 1 are consistently presented, and the theoretical derivation of the weighting is clearly tied to the method. Figures/tables and examples (results on 22 datasets, comparisons to RNN/LSTM and other baselines) appear informative, and the writing is clear with stated limitations and hyperparameters reported.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper proposes a concise, practically effective method (SIF: weighted average with weight a/(a+p(w)) plus removal of the first principal component) and provides a theoretical derivation of the weighting from a modified generative model, which is a clear new perspective compared to prior ad-hoc averaging. Empirically it shows substantial gains over unweighted averages and often matches or outperforms more complex supervised models on many benchmarks, and the authors analyze robustness and the separate contributions of weighting and common-component removal. This represents notable novelty and clear differentiation from prior work, though it is an elegant refinement of existing embedding-averaging ideas rather than a radical departure, so not scored as highly groundbreaking.

main_claims: Proposes a simple unsupervised sentence embedding method (SIF): weighted average of word vectors followed by removal of the projection on the first principal component.
Provides a theoretical justification for the SIF weighting via a modified random-walk-on-discourses generative model, deriving weights a/(a+p(w)).

Demonstrates that SIF (GloVe+WR and PSL+WR) significantly improves over unweighted average and often outperforms or matches more sophisticated supervised models (e.g., some RNN/LSTM variants) on a variety of textual similarity tasks.

Shows the method is robust to choice of weighting parameter a and to different corpora used to estimate word frequencies, and that both components (weighting and common component removal) contribute to performance gains.
_summary.methodology: Compute sentence embedding as the smooth inverse frequency (SIF) weighted average of word vectors with weight a/(a+p(w)), then compute the first principal component of sentence vectors and remove its projection from each sentence vector (Algorithm 1).
datasets: 22 textual similarity datasets including SemEval STS tasks (2012-2015)
SemEval 2014 Semantic Relatedness task (Marelli et al., 2014)
enwiki (used for training or p(w) estimation)
baselines: PP and PP-proj. (from Wieting et al., 2016)
metrics: Pearson's correlation coefficient (for textual similarity tasks)
Test accuracy (reported for SNLI and classification tasks)
limitations: Method ignores word order, which may limit performance where order matters (authors show RNN/LSTM degrade when words are shuffled).
Less advantage on supervised downstream tasks; supervised classifiers can learn to ignore common components.
Less suitable for sentiment tasks due to distributional/antonym issues and downweighting of words like "not".
Can decrease performance on some rare tasks (e.g., reported decrease on MSRpar).
